related_work_text:
5. Related Work
Recent work has explored fine-tuning LLMs for multi-step reasoning through reinforcement
learning (RL), and developing agent frameworks to structure decision-making tasks. For
reasoning, techniques range from classical PPO (Schulman et al., 2017) and actor-critic meth-
ods (Haarnoja et al., 2018) to structured prompting via meta tokens (Goyal et al., 2024; Herel
and Mikolov, 2024). Policy variants such as RLOO (Kool et al., 2019), GRPO (DeepSeek-AI et al.,
2025) and DAPO (Yu et al., 2025) could stabilize training and improve sample efficiency. Parallel
efforts like STaR (Zelikman et al., 2022) and MCTS-based reasoning (Hao et al., 2023a) promote
step-by-step reasoning with minimal supervision.
On the agent side, systems have evolved from early reactive planning (Xu et al., 2023; Yao
et al., 2022b) to modularized decision pipelines (Liu et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2023), multi-agent
cooperation (Li et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2024a), and embodied interaction (Li et al., 2025; Lin et al.,
2024b). Benchmarks like Sokoban (Junghanns and Schaeffer, 2001), FrozenLake (Dell’Aversana,
2021), and WebShop (Yao et al., 2022a) provide controlled testbeds for evaluating reasoning
under different dynamics. Our work builds on these advances, aiming to unify RL-based
reasoning with structured agent training across symbolic and language-centric tasks. We
summarize more related works in Appendix B.
